name: Aldol Condensation
summary: Aldol Condensation is a cornerstone C-C bond-forming reaction where a carbonyl compound possessing at least one alpha-hydrogen reacts to form a β-hydroxy carbonyl. The name 'Aldol' (Aldehyde + Alcohol) reflects the dual functionality. Classifications include Simple, Crossed, and Intramolecular (Cyclization). Mechanism: Base creates a nucleophilic enolate which attacks the second carbonyl. Dehydration: Heating triggers the E1cb mechanism to eliminate water and form an α,β-unsaturated carbonyl. If extended conjugation exists, this occurs spontaneously. JEE Note: Always identify the Enolate Donor vs. Carbonyl Acceptor in crossed reactions.
reactants: Aldehyde or ketone with at least one alpha-H
conditions: Cold dil. NaOH = aldol product. Heat = dehydration = condensation product.
product: Cold: beta-hydroxy carbonyl. Heat: alpha,beta-unsaturated carbonyl. Classic: CH₃CHO → crotonaldehyde (CH₃CH=CHCHO).
mechanism: Enolate attacks carbonyl C of second molecule → aldol. Heating → E1cb dehydration → alpha,beta-unsaturated product.
stereo: New stereocentre at beta-C in aldol product → racemic mixture without chiral catalyst.
mistake: Applying to HCHO or PhCHO — no alpha-H → they do Cannizzaro, NOT aldol. Also confusing the cold (aldol) and hot (condensation) products — they are different.

name: Cannizzaro Reaction
summary: Cannizzaro Reaction is a base-induced disproportionation reaction observed in aldehydes lacking alpha-hydrogens (e.g., HCHO, PhCHO). Unlike Aldol, these aldehydes cannot form enolates. Mechanism: A strong base (conc. NaOH) attacks the carbonyl carbon, eventually forcing a rate-determining Hydride Transfer (H⁻) to a second aldehyde molecule. Result: One molecule is oxidized to a carboxylate salt and the other is reduced to a primary alcohol. JEE Trap: In 'Crossed Cannizzaro' involving Formaldehyde, HCHO is ALWAYS oxidized because it is highly reactive to nucleophilic attack.
reactants: Aldehydes with NO alpha-H: HCHO, PhCHO, CCl₃CHO
reagents: Conc. NaOH (concentrated — dilute gives aldol if alpha-H present)
product: Carboxylate salt + primary alcohol. HCHO + NaOH → HCOONa + CH₃OH
mechanism: OH⁻ attacks C=O → tetrahedral alkoxide → H⁻ transfer (rate-determining) to second aldehyde → two products.
mistake: Using dilute NaOH — need CONC. In crossed Cannizzaro (HCHO + PhCHO), HCHO is ALWAYS oxidised (gives HCOO⁻), PhCHO is ALWAYS reduced (gives PhCH₂OH). Students reverse these roles.

name: Carbylamine Reaction
summary: Carbylamine Reaction (Hofmann Isocyanide Test) is a sensitive detection method for primary (1°) amines, both aliphatic and aromatic. Reaction Requirement: Must have two N-H bonds. 2° and 3° amines show no reaction. Mechanism: Chloroform and KOH generate a highly reactive Dichlorocarbene (:CCl₂) intermediate, which performs a nucleophilic attack on the amine nitrogen. Subsequent double dehydrohalogenation leads to an Isocyanide (R-NC), known for its repulsive, foul odor. Safety Note: Known as the 'Stink Test'—phenyl isocyanide is extremely toxic.
reactants: Primary amine ONLY (RNH₂) — aliphatic or aromatic
conditions: Alcoholic KOH, heat. Smell is the indicator.
product: Isocyanide (R-N≡C). Extremely foul odour = positive test.
mechanism: CHCl₃ + KOH → :CCl₂ (dichlorocarbene) → reacts with -NH₂ → R-N=CCl₂ → double HCl elimination → R-N≡C
mistake: Applying to 2° or 3° amines — NEGATIVE. Only 1° amines react. Also: isocyanide is R-N≡C (N bonded to R), NOT R-C≡N (nitrile). Structure is inverted — students draw it backwards.

name: Clemmensen Reduction
summary: Clemmensen Reduction is a deoxygenation method that converts a carbonyl group (C=O) of aldehydes and ketones directly into a methylene group (CH₂) under acidic conditions. Reagent: Zinc Amalgam [Zn(Hg)] and concentrated HCl. Mechanism: Not a simple ionic path—it occurs as a heterogeneous process on the metal surface. Note: This reaction is ideal for acid-stable substrates. If your molecule contains groups sensitive to acid (e.g., esters or ethers), this method will cause degradation—switch to Wolff-Kishner instead.
conditions: Strongly acidic (conc. HCl), heat. Substrate must be acid-stable.
product: Alkane: R-CO-R' → R-CH₂-R'. C=O becomes CH₂.
mechanism: Heterogeneous surface mechanism on Zn. Not a simple ionic pathway.
mistake: Using for acid-sensitive substrates — they are destroyed. Use Wolff-Kishner (basic) instead. Also: does NOT reduce COOH or esters — only aldehyde/ketone C=O.

name: Diazotisation
summary: Diazotisation is the preparation of aromatic diazonium salts (ArN₂⁺Cl⁻), which serve as essential intermediates for synthesizing phenols, halides, and azo dyes. Requirement: Primary aromatic amines reacting with NaNO₂ and HCl at 0-5°C. Mechanism: Generation of the Nitrosonium ion (NO⁺) followed by N-nitrosation and subsequent dehydration. Critical Constraint: The temperature must be strictly below 5°C. Above this range, the salt decomposes into phenol with the evolution of N₂ gas. Aliphatic amines give unstable diazoniums that instantly form carbocations.
reactants: Primary aromatic amine (ArNH₂): aniline, substituted anilines
conditions: ICE COLD: 0–5°C. Above 5°C → diazonium decomposes to phenol + N₂.
mechanism: HNO₂ + ArNH₂ → N-nitrosoamine → tautomerism → ArN₂⁺
mistake: Using aliphatic amines — alkyl diazonium instantly decomposes to alcohol + N₂. Only AROMATIC amines form stable diazonium. Also: above 5°C → decomposition to phenol.

name: Friedel-Crafts Acylation
summary: Friedel-Crafts Acylation is the introduction of an acyl group (R-CO-) into an aromatic ring to form an aryl ketone. Electrophile: The resonance-stabilized Acylium ion (R-C≡O⁺), generated from acyl chloride and AlCl₃. Advantages: Unlike alkylation, this reaction has no carbocation rearrangement and no polyacylation (the product deactivates the ring). Note: $>1$ equivalent of AlCl₃ is required because the product carbonyl complexes with the catalyst. Deactivated rings (like Nitrobenzene) do not react.
reactants: Benzene + acyl chloride (RCOCl) or acid anhydride
reagents: Anhydrous AlCl₃ (>1 equiv — one equiv complexes with product C=O)
conditions: Anhydrous. Excess AlCl₃ needed because product ketone complexes with AlCl₃.
product: Aryl ketone: PhH + CH₃COCl + AlCl₃ → PhCOCH₃ (acetophenone) + HCl
mechanism: RCOCl + AlCl₃ → RCO⁺ (acylium — resonance-stabilised → no rearrangement). EAS. Product C=O complexes AlCl₃ → ring deactivated → no polyacylation.
mistake: Forgetting that >1 equiv AlCl₃ is needed. Acylation also FAILS on deactivated rings. Best way to put a long-chain alkyl on a ring without rearrangement: FC acylation → Clemmensen/WK.

name: Friedel-Crafts Alkylation
summary: Friedel-Crafts Alkylation is an electrophilic aromatic substitution used to attach an alkyl group to a benzene ring. Mechanism: An alkyl halide and Lewis acid (AlCl₃) generate a carbocation electrophile. Limitations: (1) Carbocation rearrangement can lead to unexpected products (e.g., n-Propyl bromide yielding isopropylbenzene). (2) Polyalkylation occurs because the alkyl group activates the ring toward further attack. (3) Failing on deactivated rings. Standard strategy: Perform FC acylation first followed by reduction to avoid these issues.
conditions: Anhydrous AlCl₃, anhydrous conditions. H₂O destroys catalyst.
product: Alkylbenzene: PhH + CH₃Cl + AlCl₃ → PhCH₃ + HCl
mechanism: RX + AlCl₃ → R⁺ + AlCl₄⁻. R⁺ attacks benzene (EAS) → sigma complex → H⁺ loss → product.
mistake: Three JEE limitations: (1) REARRANGEMENT — 1° carbocations rearrange (n-PrCl + Ph + AlCl₃ → isopropylbenzene, NOT n-propylbenzene). (2) POLYALKYLATION — product is more reactive than benzene. (3) FAILS on deactivated rings (nitrobenzene, etc.).

name: Grignard Reaction
summary: Grignard Reaction involves the nucleophilic addition of an organomagnesium halide (RMgX) to a carbonyl compound to form a new C-C bond. Polarity: The C-Mg bond is highly polarized, making the carbon an extremely strong nucleophile/base. Outcomes: Formaldehyde gives 1° alcohols, higher aldehydes give 2° alcohols, and ketones give 3° alcohols. Golden Rule: Reaction must be strictly anhydrous. Protic sources (H₂O, alcohols, -NH₂, -COOH) 'kill' the Grignard reagent instantly, reducing it to a simple alkane (R-H).
reactants: RMgX + HCHO → 1° OH  |  RCHO → 2° OH  |  R₂CO → 3° OH  |  CO₂ → RCOOH  |  Ester → 3° OH (2 moles RMgX)
reagents: Mg in dry ether for prep. Then carbonyl. Then H₃O⁺ workup.
conditions: Strictly anhydrous and aprotic. Dry ether or THF. One drop of water destroys Grignard: RMgX + H₂O → RH.
product: 1° OH (HCHO), 2° OH (RCHO), 3° OH (R₂CO), RCOOH (CO₂), 3° OH same R (ester)
mechanism: C(δ−)-Mg(δ+): C is nucleophile. Attacks electrophilic carbonyl C → Mg-alkoxide → H₃O⁺ → alcohol.
stereo: New stereocentre at former carbonyl C → RACEMIC mixture (attack from both faces).
mistake: Using with ANY protic compound (H₂O, ROH, NH₃, RCOOH, RNH₂) — IMMEDIATELY DESTROYED → gives only RH. Also with esters: 2 moles of RMgX react (first → ketone intermediate, second → 3° alcohol with R added twice).

name: Hofmann Bromamide Reaction
summary: Hofmann Bromamide Degradation is a C-C bond-shortening rearrangement of a primary amide into a primary amine with one fewer carbon atom. Active Species: Sodium hypobromite (NaOBr), formed in situ from Br₂ and NaOH. Mechanism: Formation of an N-bromoamide followed by deprotonation to trigger a 1,2-rearrangement of the alkyl/aryl group from carbonyl carbon to nitrogen. The resulting Isocyanate (R-N=C=O) is hydrolyzed to release the amine and CO₂. Configuration: The migrating group (R) always retains its stereochemical configuration.
reactants: Primary amide (RCONH₂) ONLY. Does not work for 2° or 3° amides.
reagents: Br₂ + NaOH (NaOBr is the active species)
product: RNH₂ (n-1 carbons): CH₃CONH₂ → CH₃NH₂. CO₂ is lost.
mechanism: NaOBr → N-bromoamide → base removes N-H → Br⁻ leaves → isocyanate (R-N=C=O) → H₂O → carbamic acid → CO₂ + RNH₂
stereo: Configuration at R is RETAINED (rearrangement at carbonyl, not at R).
mistake: Writing the same number of carbons in product as in starting amide — WRONG. Amide has n C, amine has n-1 C. CO₂ escapes! Also: confusing with Hofmann ELIMINATION (quaternary salt → least substituted alkene) — completely different reaction.

name: Haloform / Iodoform Reaction
summary: Haloform (Iodoform) Reaction is a specific test for methyl ketones (CH₃-CO-) and alcohols oxidizable to them (e.g., ethanol). Condition: Iodine and NaOH generate sodium hypoiodite (NaOI). Mechanism: Base-induced trihalogenation of the methyl group followed by a nucleophilic attack on the carbonyl, which cleaves the molecule to form Iodoform (CHI₃) and a carboxylate salt. Diagnostic Result: Formation of a bright yellow crystalline precipitate of CHI₃ with a characteristic medicinal odor. Propan-1-ol and Methanol give negative results.
reactants: CH₃COR (methyl ketone), CH₃CHO, ethanol, isopropanol (all oxidised to methyl carbonyl in situ)
conditions: Alkaline medium (NaOH), room temperature. Yellow ppt = positive.
product: CHI₃ (yellow iodoform ppt) + RCOONa (one fewer carbon than starting material)
mechanism: Step 1: all 3 alpha-H on methyl replaced by I (trihalogenation). Step 2: OH⁻ cleaves C-C → CHI₃ + RCOO⁻
mistake: ALL ketones give iodoform — FALSE. Only METHYL ketones (CH₃CO-). Ethanol → YES, Isopropanol → YES (oxidised to acetone), n-Propanol → NO (gives propanal, no CH₃CO-), Diethyl ketone → NO.

name: Kolbe's Reaction (Kolbe-Schmitt)
summary: Kolbe-Schmitt Reaction is the carboxylation of a phenol to form an ortho-hydroxy aromatic acid (Salicylic acid). Requirement: Phenol must be converted to Sodium Phenoxide first to activate the ring. Mechanism: CO₂, a weak electrophile, performs an electrophilic substitution at the ortho position, stabilized by a chelate-like coordination with the sodium cation. Industrial Importance: This is the primary commercial route to Salicylic acid, the precursor to Aspirin. Note: Potassium Phenoxide at higher temperatures favors the para-product.
reagents: CO₂ gas at 4–5 atm, then H⁺ workup
conditions: 125°C, 4–5 atm CO₂. Na-phenoxide → ortho. K-phenoxide at 250°C → para (p-hydroxybenzoic acid).
product: Salicylic acid (2-hydroxybenzoic acid). Aspirin = salicylic acid + acetic anhydride.
mechanism: CO₂ (weak electrophile) attacks ortho of electron-rich phenoxide ring → H⁺ workup → salicylic acid.
mistake: Temperature and cation matter: Na + 125°C = ORTHO. K + 250°C = PARA. Using free phenol (need the Na-salt). Students mix up temperature and product.

name: Reimer-Tiemann Reaction
summary: Reimer-Tiemann Reaction is the formylation of phenols to yield Salicylaldehyde. Transition Species: Dichlorocarbene (:CCl₂), generated in situ from Chloroform and concentrated NaOH. Mechanism: The neutral but electron-deficient carbene attacks the nucleophilic phenoxide ring at the ortho position (major). Subsequent hydrolysis of the gem-dichloride intermediate yields the formyl (-CHO) group. Variation: Using Carbon Tetrachloride (CCl₄) instead of Chloroform yields Salicylic Acid instead of an aldehyde.
mechanism: CHCl₃ + NaOH → :CCl₂ (dichlorocarbene) → attacks ortho of phenoxide → gem-dichloride intermediate + 2NaOH → CHO + 2NaCl (hydrolysis).
mistake: Writing CHO⁺ as the electrophile — WRONG. The electrophile is :CCl₂ (dichlorocarbene). Also: works ONLY for phenols — not plain benzene (use Gattermann-Koch for benzene).

name: Rosenmund Reduction
summary: Rosenmund Reduction is the selective catalytic hydrogenation of an acyl chloride to an aldehyde. Reagent: Palladium catalyst supported on Barium Sulphate (Pd/BaSO₄), poisoned with sulfur or quinoline. The Poison Logic: Un-poisoned Pd is too active and would reduce the resulting aldehyde all the way to an alcohol. The poison selectively deactivates the catalyst just enough to stop at the R-CHO stage. Constraint: It cannot be used to synthesize Formaldehyde (since Formyl Chloride is unstable).
mechanism: Surface hydrogenation on poisoned Pd. Stops at aldehyde — un-poisoned Pd would reduce to alcohol.
mistake: Using normal Pd/C — over-reduces to primary alcohol. Pd/BaSO₄ (POISONED) is essential. Also: LiAlH₄ or NaBH₄ cannot stop at aldehyde.

name: Sandmeyer Reaction
summary: Sandmeyer Reaction is a radical-nucleophilic substitution used to replace an aromatic diazonium group with Cl, Br, or CN. Reagent: Cuprous salts (CuCl, CuBr, CuCN). Mechanism: A Single Electron Transfer (SET) occurs between the Cu(I) salt and the diazonium cation, releasing N₂ gas and generating a reactive aryl radical. This radical then captures the halogen/nitrile from the copper complex. Importance: This reaction provides a versatile route to functionalize benzene rings in patterns not possible through standard EAS.
reagents: CuCl → ArCl; CuBr → ArBr; CuCN → ArCN. For ArF: Balz-Schiemann (HBF₄). For ArI: KI directly (no Cu needed). For ArOH: H₂O/heat. For ArH: H₃PO₂.
conditions: Cu(I) salts, mild heat. Balz-Schiemann: HBF₄ then heat dry diazonium tetrafluoroborate.
product: ArCl, ArBr, ArCN, ArF, ArI, ArOH, ArH depending on reagent.
mechanism: Cu(I) radical mechanism: Cu(I) → Cu(II) + e⁻ → Ar• → Ar-X. NOT a simple SN2.
mistake: Using Cu for ArI (use KI directly) or ArF (use Balz-Schiemann). Also: Sandmeyer is for AROMATIC diazonium — aliphatic diazonium is too unstable.

name: Stephen Reduction
summary: Stephen Reduction is the selective conversion of nitriles to aldehydes using Tin(II) chloride and HCl. Process Flow: (1) Nitrile is reduced to an iminium salt (imine hydrochloride) by SnCl₂/HCl. (2) Subsequent steam distillation or aqueous hydrolysis of the intermediate yields the final aldehyde. Distinction: Unlike strong hydrides (LiAlH₄) which reduce nitriles to primary amines, the Stephen reduction is 'mild' and stops specifically at the aldehyde stage. Typical Example: CH₃CN (Acetonitrile) $\rightarrow$ CH₃CHO (Acetaldehyde).
conditions: Anhydrous HCl + SnCl₂, then aqueous workup
mechanism: SnCl₂ reduces C≡N to C=NH (imine) as Sn(II) complex → H₂O hydrolysis → RCHO + NH₃
mistake: Confusing with Rosenmund (RCOCl → RCHO) — both give aldehyde but from different starting materials. Stephen: nitrile → aldehyde (same C count). Also: LiAlH₄ on nitrile gives PRIMARY AMINE (not aldehyde).

name: Wolff-Kishner Reduction
summary: Wolff-Kishner Reduction is the complete deoxygenation of aldehydes and ketones to alkanes under strongly basic conditions. Reagent: Hydrazine (NH₂NH₂) and a strong base (KOH/NaOH) in a high-boiling solvent like ethylene glycol. Mechanism: Formation of a hydrazone intermediate followed by base-induced elimination of N₂ gas—the irreversible loss of nitrogen provides a massive thermodynamic driving force. Benefit: This is the ideal alternative to Clemmensen reduction when the molecule contains acid-sensitive functional groups.
reagents: NH₂NH₂ (hydrazine) + KOH in ethylene glycol (high boiling point solvent)
conditions: High temperature (~200°C), strongly basic (KOH). Substrate must be base-stable.
product: Alkane: R-CO-R' → R-CH₂-R'. Same as Clemmensen but under basic conditions.
mechanism: C=O + NH₂NH₂ → hydrazone (C=N-NH₂) → KOH/heat → N₂ loss → carbanion → protonation → CH₂
mistake: Using for base-sensitive substrates. Use Clemmensen (acid) instead. Also: Huang Minlon modification (aqueous KOH + diethylene glycol) is a variant — same product.

name: Fischer Esterification
summary: Fischer Esterification is an acid-catalyzed reversible condensation between a carboxylic acid and an alcohol. Mechanism: Protonation of the carbonyl oxygen makes the carbon more electrophilic, facilitating nucleophilic attack by the alcohol. Evidence: ¹⁸O isotope labeling proves that the 'O' in the resulting ester comes from the alcohol, while the 'O' in the water byproduct comes from the acid. Le Chatelier: Since the reaction is an equilibrium, it is driven forward by using excess alcohol or continuously removing water.
reagents: H⁺ catalyst (conc. H₂SO₄ or dry HCl), heat
conditions: Reversible. Drive forward: excess alcohol, remove water (distillation/molecular sieves), or conc. H₂SO₄.
product: Ester (RCOOR') + H₂O: CH₃COOH + C₂H₅OH ⇌ CH₃COOC₂H₅ + H₂O
mechanism: H⁺ protonates C=O → R'OH attacks → tetrahedral intermediate → H₂O loss → ester. Acyl-oxygen cleavage (confirmed by ¹⁸O labelling).
mistake: Forgetting it is REVERSIBLE. Also: ¹⁸O labelling shows the O in ester comes from the ALCOHOL (not the acid). The acid loses its -OH as water. Tertiary alcohols react poorly (elimination competes).

name: Gabriel Phthalimide Synthesis
summary: Gabriel Phthalimide Synthesis is a highly reliable method for preparing pure Primary (1°) Amines without risk of over-alkylation. Concept: It avoids the messy mixtures of ammonia alkylation by using Phthalimide as a 'masked' nitrogen source. Process: (1) Deprotonation of Phthalimide by KOH ($pK_a \approx 8$). (2) SN2 attack on a primary alkyl halide. (3) Hydrazinolysis or hydrolysis to release the amine. Limitation: Aryl halides and 3° alkyl halides fail to react as they do not undergo SN2 substitution under these conditions.
reagents: Step 1: KOH. Step 2: RX. Step 3: acid hydrolysis (H₃O⁺) or hydrazinolysis (N₂H₄).
conditions: Anhydrous for SN2 step; then acidic hydrolysis or hydrazinolysis.
mechanism: Phthalimide N-H (pKa ~8.3) deprotonated by KOH → N-nucleophile → SN2 on RX → N-alkyl phthalimide → hydrolysis → RNH₂.
stereo: SN2 step: inversion of configuration at RX carbon if chiral.
mistake: Gabriel cannot make secondary or tertiary amines — phthalimide has ONE N-H so only one alkyl group attaches. Also: primary aryl amines cannot be made (ArX doesn't undergo SN2 easily).

name: Williamson Ether Synthesis
summary: Williamson Ether Synthesis is an SN2-based laboratory method for synthesizing both symmetrical and unsymmetrical ethers. Requirement: A nucleophilic alkoxide (RO⁻) reacting with an alkyl halide (R'X). Golden Rule: The alkyl halide MUST be primary (1°). If a 3° halide is used, the alkoxide acts as a strong base instead of a nucleophile, leading to E2 elimination (forming an alkene) rather than substitution. Strategic Tip: Always place the more sterically hindered (bulky) group on the alkoxide side, not the halide side.
reagents: Na metal + alcohol → alkoxide, then RX. Or NaOH + phenol → phenoxide + RX.
mechanism: SN2: alkoxide O⁻ attacks back of C-X → concerted → ether + X⁻
stereo: Inversion of configuration at RX carbon (SN2).
mistake: Using tertiary RX — SN2 fails, elimination dominates. SWAP ROLES: tertiary group on the ALKOXIDE side. e.g. for (CH₃)₃C-O-CH₃: use (CH₃)₃CONa + CH₃I, NOT (CH₃)₃CI + CH₃ONa.

name: Azo Coupling Reaction
summary: Azo Coupling is an electrophilic aromatic substitution reaction between an aromatic diazonium cation and an activated aromatic ring (like phenol or aniline). Target: Formation of highly colored 'Azo Dyes' ($Ar-N=N-Ar'$). pH Sensitivity: Phenols react best in weakly basic medium ($pH \, 9-10$), while anilines react best in weakly acidic medium ($pH \, 4-5$). Logic: The diazonium ion is a weak electrophile and requires a high-electron-density ring to attack. Substitution occurs exclusively at the para-position (or ortho if para is blocked).
reactants: Diazonium salt (electrophile) + phenol or aromatic amine (activated ring)
conditions: Phenol: mildly alkaline (pH 8–10, forms PhO⁻). Amine: weakly acidic/neutral. Temperature: 0–5°C always.
product: Azo compound (Ar-N=N-Ar') — coloured dye. Para coupling major product.
mechanism: ArN₂⁺ (weak electrophile) attacks para of activated ring (EAS) → sigma complex → H⁺ loss → azo dye.
stereo: E/Z isomerism about N=N bond. E (trans) = thermodynamically more stable.
mistake: Phenol coupling needs MILD BASE (PhO⁻ = more activated). Amine coupling needs WEAK ACID (strong acid protonates -NH₂ → -NH₃⁺ = deactivated ring). Students swap the pH conditions.

name: Balz-Schiemann Reaction
summary: Balz-Schiemann Reaction is the standard method for synthesizing aryl fluorides from diazonium salts. Process: (1) Treatment of an aryl diazonium salt with fluoroboric acid ($HBF_4$) to precipitate stable Aryl Diazonium Tetrafluoroborate ($ArN_2BF_4$). (2) Controlled thermal decomposition of the dry salt to yield $Ar-F$, $N_2$, and $BF_3$. Distinction: This is the only reliable way to introduce fluorine into a benzene ring, as direct fluorination is too violent and standard Sandmeyer ($CuF$) fails.
reagents: HBF₄ or NaBF₄, then dry heat
conditions: Step 1: form ArN₂⁺BF₄⁻ (precipitates). Step 2: dry pyrolysis of dry salt → ArF.
product: Aryl fluoride (ArF) e.g. fluorobenzene from aniline.
mechanism: Thermal decomposition of diazonium fluoroborate → aryl cation + N₂ + BF₄⁻ → ArF + BF₃
mistake: Trying Sandmeyer for ArF — poor results. Must use Balz-Schiemann. It is a TWO-step process: form BF₄⁻ salt FIRST (precipitates out), THEN dry pyrolysis.

name: Beckmann Rearrangement
summary: Beckmann Rearrangement is the acid-catalyzed conversion of an oxime into an amide. Driving Force: The migration of a group from carbon to nitrogen, simultaneous with the loss of a leaving group from nitrogen. Stereochemistry: The reaction is 'Stereospecific'—the group that is **anti** (trans) to the leaving group (-OH) is the one that migrates. Application: The rearrangement of cyclohexanone oxime yields $\epsilon$-caprolactam, the essential monomer for synthesizing Nylon-6. Catalysts include $H_2SO_4$, $PCl_5$, or $SOCl_2$.
conditions: Strong acid conditions. For cyclic ketones: ring expansion (cyclohexanone oxime → caprolactam, the nylon-6 precursor).
product: Amide (R-CO-NHR') or lactam. The group ANTI to -OH migrates.
mechanism: H⁺ protonates oxime -OH → H₂O leaves → nitrilium ion → anti-group migrates to N (1,2-shift) → H₂O attacks → amide.
stereo: The group ANTI (trans) to -OH in the oxime migrates. Stereospecific: different oxime isomers give different amide products.
mistake: Thinking either group can migrate — ONLY the anti group (trans to OH) migrates. Also: cyclic ketone → ring EXPANSION (cyclohexanone → 7-membered lactam, caprolactam). Students expect ring contraction.

name: Benzoin Condensation
summary: Benzoin Condensation is the cyanide-catalyzed coupling of two aromatic aldehyde molecules (e.g., Benzaldehyde) to form an $\alpha$-hydroxy ketone (Benzoin). Catalyst: Aqueous-alcoholic sodium or potassium cyanide ($NaCN/KCN$). Specificity: Only aromatic aldehydes (lacking alpha-H) typically undergo this condensation. Crucial Role of $CN^-$: It acts as both a nucleophile (to form a cyanohydrin intermediate) and an exceptionally good leaving group. Note: Unlike Aldol, this reaction creates a new C-C bond between two carbonyl carbons.
reagents: KCN (potassium cyanide) or NaCN; aqueous ethanol; heat
mechanism: CN⁻ adds to PhCHO → cyanohydrin → anion (umpolung) → attacks second PhCHO → rearrangement → CN⁻ expelled → benzoin
stereo: Racemic benzoin formed (no stereocontrol with simple KCN).
mistake: Confusing with aldol — benzaldehyde has NO alpha-H so it cannot do aldol. Benzoin uses CN⁻ catalyst. Aliphatic aldehydes do NOT give benzoin condensation with KCN.

name: Birch Reduction
summary: Birch Reduction is the selective partial reduction of aromatic rings to non-conjugated 1,4-cyclohexadienes. Reagent: Sodium or Lithium metal in liquid ammonia ($NH_3$) with a proton source (usually an alcohol like Ethanol). Mechanism: Single Electron Transfer (SET) to form radical anions. Substituent Effect: Electron-Withdrawing Groups (EWG) like $-COOH$ direct reduction to the 1,4-positions (ipso-para), while Electron-Donating Groups (EDG) like $-OCH_3$ direct it to the 2,5-positions (ortho-meta).
reactants: Benzene or substituted benzene (EDG or EWG)
reagents: Na or Li metal + liquid NH₃ (−33°C) + ROH (proton source)
conditions: Liquid ammonia (−33°C), Na metal, alcohol as proton source essential.
product: 1,4-cyclohexadiene. Position of double bonds depends on substituents.
mechanism: Na → solvated e⁻ in NH₃ → radical anion → protonation → second e⁻ → anion → protonation → 1,4-diene.
stereo: EDG: double bonds ON substituted carbons (EDG stays on C=C). EWG: double bonds AWAY from EWG (EWG on sp³ carbon).
mistake: Getting regioselectivity wrong: EDG → double bond ON those carbons. EWG → double bond AWAY. Also: result is 1,4-DIENE, not cyclohexane. Alcohol as proton source is essential.

name: Claisen Condensation
summary: Claisen Condensation is a C-C bond-forming reaction between two esters (or one ester and one carbonyl) accompanied by the loss of an alcohol. Requirement: The starting ester must possess two alpha-hydrogens to drive the reaction to completion via a final deprotonation. Reagent: A strong base, typically the sodium alkoxide ($NaOR$) matching the ester's alcohol part. Result: A $\beta$-keto ester. Thermodynamic Logic: The reaction is equilibrium-driven; the final irreversible deprotonation of the acidic alpha-proton between the two carbonyls is what forces the yield.
reactants: Ester (requires alpha-H for the donor). Two molecules of same ester (Claisen) or different esters (crossed Claisen).
reagents: NaOEt (sodium ethoxide, strong base) in dry ethanol
product: Beta-ketoester: 2 CH₃COOEt + NaOEt → CH₃COCH₂COOEt (ethyl acetoacetate) + EtOH
mechanism: NaOEt deprotonates alpha-C of ester → enolate → attacks carbonyl C of second ester → tetrahedral intermediate → EtO⁻ expelled → beta-ketoester
mistake: Confusing with aldol (which involves aldehyde/ketone + NaOH). Claisen = ESTER + NaOEt. Also: one ester must have no alpha-H to act as acceptor in crossed Claisen, otherwise mixture results.

name: Decarboxylation (Soda Lime)
summary: Decarboxylation is the removal of the carboxyl group ($-COOH$) from a carboxylic acid as $CO_2$. Common Method: Heating the sodium salt of the acid with Soda Lime ($NaOH + CaO$ in 3:1 ratio). Mechanism: Formation of a carbanion intermediate. Stability Rule: The rate of decarboxylation depends on the stability of the resulting carbanion. $\beta$-keto acids are exceptionally prone to decarboxylation—they lose $CO_2$ simply upon heating via a cyclic 6-membered transition state, bypassing the need for soda lime.
reagents: Soda lime = NaOH + CaO (1:3 ratio), strong heat (300°C+)
conditions: Strong heat. CaO prevents NaOH melting and activates it.
product: Alkane (n-1 C): CH₃COONa + soda lime → CH₄ + Na₂CO₃
mechanism: CaO activates carboxylate → decarboxylation → carbanion → protonation → alkane
mistake: NaOH alone is insufficient — need soda lime (NaOH + CaO). Confusing with Kolbe electrolysis: Kolbe = 2RCOO⁻ → R-R (coupling, gains C). Soda lime = RCOOH → RH (loses 1 C). Completely opposite.

name: Etard Reaction
summary: Etard Reaction is the selective partial oxidation of a methyl group on an aromatic ring to an aldehyde group. Reagent: Chromyl chloride ($CrO_2Cl_2$) in a non-polar solvent like $CS_2$ or $CCl_4$. Process: (1) Formation of a brown-colored Etard complex (an adduct of chromyl chloride and the hydrocarbon). (2) Careful hydrolysis of this complex to release the aldehyde. Precision: This method is highly valued because it stops specifically at the aldehyde stage without over-oxidizing to the carboxylic acid (benzoic acid).
reactants: Toluene (PhCH₃) or alkylbenzene with methyl group
conditions: CCl₄ solvent, room temperature. Then H₂O workup.
mechanism: CrO₂Cl₂ forms chromate ester with methyl → hydrolysis gives aldehyde selectively.
mistake: Confusing with KMnO₄ which burns toluene methyl all the way to COOH. CrO₂Cl₂ in CCl₄ = selective STOP at CHO. KMnO₄ = COOH.

name: Finkelstein Reaction
summary: Finkelstein Reaction is a halogen exchange method ($S_N2$) used to synthesize alkyl iodides. Reagent: Sodium Iodide ($NaI$) in dry acetone. Mechanism: An alkyl chloride or bromide is treated with $NaI$. Thermodynamic Driver: While the reaction is an equilibrium, it is driven to completion by the precipitation of the resulting $NaCl$ or $NaBr$, which are insoluble in dry acetone (consistent with Le Chatelier's principle). Note: This is an $S_N2$ reaction, so reactivity follows the order: $1^\circ > 2^\circ > 3^\circ$ (3° halides typically fail).
conditions: Dry acetone. NaCl/NaBr precipitate out → equilibrium shifts forward.
product: Alkyl iodide (RI): RCl + NaI → RI + NaCl↓
mechanism: SN2: I⁻ attacks C-Cl (backside). NaCl insoluble in acetone → precipitates → drives equilibrium.
mistake: Reaction done in water — NaCl soluble in water → equilibrium doesn't shift → doesn't work. Dry acetone is essential. Also fails for 3° RX (SN2 impossible).

name: Fries Rearrangement
summary: Fries Rearrangement is the phenolic ester rearrangement into ortho and para-hydroxy aryl ketones. Reagent: Anhydrous $AlCl_3$. Temperature Control: Law of the land—Low temperatures ($<60^\circ C$) favor the **para** product, while high temperatures ($>160^\circ C$) favor the **ortho** product due to intramolecular hydrogen bonding stability. Mechanism: It involves the formation of an acylium ion intermediate which then re-attacks the benzene ring at the available ortho/para sites.
conditions: Low temp → para product. High temp → ortho product.
product: Hydroxy aryl ketone: PhOCOR + AlCl₃ → o-HOC₆H₄COR (major at high T) + p-HOC₆H₄COR (major at low T)
mechanism: AlCl₃ complexes with ester C=O → acylium migrates onto phenol ring (EAS) → ortho or para ketone
mistake: Temperature selectivity is inverted vs intuition: HIGH temperature = ORTHO (thermodynamic control). LOW temperature = PARA (kinetic control). Students mix these up.

name: Gattermann-Koch Reaction
summary: Gattermann-Koch Reaction is a direct formylation method used to synthesize aromatic aldehydes from benzene or its derivatives. Reagents: Carbon Monoxide ($CO$) and Hydrogen Chloride ($HCl$) in the presence of anhydrous $AlCl_3$ and Cuprous Chloride ($CuCl$). Active Electrophile: The formyl cation ($[CHO]^+$), generated in situ. Note: This reaction is effectively an 'Acylation' with an unstable formyl chloride equivalent. It bypasses the need to handle unstable formaldehyde common in other formylation methods.
reagents: CO + HCl gas, anhydrous AlCl₃, CuCl catalyst, high pressure
conditions: Anhydrous AlCl₃, CuCl, CO + HCl, high pressure. Fails on deactivated rings.
mechanism: CO + HCl + AlCl₃/CuCl → formyl cation (HCO⁺) → EAS on benzene → benzaldehyde.
mistake: Thinking it works on phenol (use Reimer-Tiemann for phenol). G-K is for BENZENE rings. Also fails on deactivated rings.

name: Hell-Volhard-Zelinsky (HVZ)
summary: Hell-Volhard-Zelinsky (HVZ) Reaction is the specific $\alpha$-halogenation of aliphatic carboxylic acids. Requirement: The acid must possess at least one $\alpha$-hydrogen. Reagents: Chlorine or Bromine in the presence of a catalytic amount of Red Phosphorus ($P_{red}$). Mechanism: Phosphorus reacts with the halogen to form $PX_3$, which converts the acid to an acid halide. This acid halide undergoes enolization and halogenation at the alpha-carbon more readily than the parent acid. Result: $\alpha$-halo carboxylic acids.
conditions: P + Br₂ → PBr₃ in situ → RCOOH → RCOBr → enolisation → Br₂ at alpha-C → H₂O workup.
product: Alpha-bromo carboxylic acid: CH₃COOH → BrCH₂COOH (bromoacetic acid)
mechanism: P + Br₂ → PBr₃ → RCOOH → RCOBr → enol → Br₂ at alpha → alpha-bromo acyl bromide → H₂O → alpha-bromo acid.
stereo: New stereocentre at alpha-C → racemic mixture (enol face attack from both sides).
mistake: Applying to ketones or aldehydes — HVZ is SPECIFICALLY for carboxylic acids. Without phosphorus, RCOOH barely reacts with Br₂. Phosphorus is essential.

name: Perkin Reaction
summary: Perkin Reaction is an aldol-like condensation between an aromatic aldehyde (e.g., Benzaldehyde) and an acid anhydride. Reagent: The sodium salt of the carboxylic acid corresponding to the anhydride. Mechanism: The base deprotonates the anhydride to form an enolate, which performs a nucleophilic addition to the aromatic aldehyde. Subsequent dehydration and hydrolysis yield an $\alpha,\beta$-unsaturated aromatic acid. Benchmark Product: Cinnamic acid ($Ph-CH=CH-COOH$).
reagents: Acetic anhydride (Ac₂O), sodium acetate (NaOAc, mild base)
conditions: Heat 150–200°C. NaOAc acts as mild base.
product: Trans-cinnamic acid: PhCHO + Ac₂O → Ph-CH=CH-COOH + CH₃COOH
mechanism: NaOAc deprotonates Ac₂O at alpha → enolate attacks ArCHO → beta-hydroxy → dehydration → alpha,beta-unsaturated acid.
stereo: Trans (E) isomer is major product (thermodynamic control).
mistake: Confusing with aldol — aldol uses NaOH, Perkin uses NaOAc + ACID ANHYDRIDE as carbon source. Aliphatic aldehydes work poorly.

name: Pinacol-Pinacolone Rearrangement
summary: Pinacol-Pinacolone Rearrangement is an acid-catalyzed rearrangement of a vicinal diol (1,2-diol) to a ketone or aldehyde. Key Steps: (1) Protonation of one hydroxyl group and its departure as water, forming a carbocation. (2) A 1,2-shift of an alkyl or aryl group from an adjacent carbon to the carbocation center, driven by the formation of a more stable oxocarbenium ion. (3) Deprotonation yields the final carbonyl product. Migration Aptitude: Aryl $>$ Alkyl (with $3^\circ > 2^\circ > 1^\circ$ for alkyl groups).
mechanism: H⁺ protonates one OH → H₂O leaves → tertiary carbocation → 1,2-methyl shift → oxocarbenium ion stabilised by O lone pair → deprotonation → ketone.
stereo: The migrating group is anti-periplanar to the leaving group in the concerted version.
mistake: Thinking the less stable carbocation forms first — no, one OH is protonated and leaves to give the MORE stable initial carbocation. Then migration gives an even more stable oxocarbenium. Students mix up which carbon loses OH first.

name: Wurtz Reaction
summary: Wurtz Reaction is a method for synthesizing symmetrical alkanes ($R-R$) by coupling two molecules of an alkyl halide ($RX$). Reagents: Sodium metal ($Na$) in dry ether. Mechanism: It proceeds via a free radical pathway or an organosodium intermediate ($R-Na$), which then reacts with another alkyl halide molecule in an $S_N2$-like fashion. Limitations: Poor yields for unsymmetrical alkanes (due to mixture of products), and it fails for $3^\circ$ halides which undergo elimination.
reactants: Alkyl halide (RX) — primary best. Tertiary fails (elimination).
mechanism: RX + 2Na → RNa (organosodium) + NaX; RNa + RX → R-R + NaX
mistake: Using a single RX to make an odd-carbon alkane — impossible. Single halide always gives even-carbon product. Mixed Wurtz (two different RX) gives three products (symmetrical + unsymmetrical alkane mixture).

name: Wurtz-Fittig Reaction
summary: Wurtz-Fittig Reaction is a cross-coupling reaction between an aryl halide ($ArX$) and an alkyl halide ($RX$) to form an alkylarene ($Ar-R$). Reagents: Sodium metal ($Na$) in dry ether. Advantage: It combines the features of the Wurtz reaction (alkyl-alkyl coupling) and the Fittig reaction (aryl-aryl coupling) to specifically form an alkyl-aryl product. The different reactivities of aryl and alkyl halides allow for better control over the cross-coupling compared to a mixed Wurtz reaction.
mechanism: Organosodium intermediate; aryl and alkyl groups couple
mistake: Three-way confusion: Wurtz = alkyl+alkyl → alkane. Fittig = aryl+aryl → biaryl. Wurtz-Fittig = aryl+alkyl → alkylarene.

name: Dow Process (Chlorobenzene Hydrolysis)
summary: Dow Process is an industrial nucleophilic aromatic substitution method for synthesizing phenol from chlorobenzene. Requirement: Extreme conditions ($350^\circ C$ and $300$ atm pressure) are necessary because chlorobenzene is deactivated toward nucleophilic attack due to partial double bond character of the C-Cl bond. Intermediate: It proceeds via a Benzyne intermediate ($C_6H_4$) rather than a simple $S_N2$ or $S_NAr$ pathway. It remains a classic example of forcing a reaction on an unreactive substrate.
mechanism: Benzyne (aryne) mechanism: E2-like elimination of HCl → benzyne → OH⁻ addition → sodium phenoxide → H⁺ → phenol.
mistake: Treating it as simple SN2 on benzene — benzene rings do NOT do SN2. The mechanism goes through benzyne (aryne). Harsh conditions (350°C, 300 atm) are essential.

name: Kolbe Electrolysis
summary: Kolbe Electrolysis is an electrochemical method for synthesizing higher symmetrical alkanes from the salts of carboxylic acids. Process: Electrolysis of an aqueous solution of sodium or potassium carboxylate ($R-COONa$). Mechanism: At the ANODE, the carboxylate ion is oxidized to a radical ($R-COO^\bullet$), which loses $CO_2$ to form an alkyl radical ($R^\bullet$). Two such radicals then couple. Observations: $CO_2$ and Alkane are evolved at the anode, while $H_2$ gas and $NaOH$ are formed at the cathode, making the solution basic over time.
conditions: Aqueous RCOONa solution, electrolysis, Pt anode
product: Symmetrical alkane: 2RCOO⁻ → R-R + 2CO₂ + 2e⁻ (at anode). e.g. 2CH₃COO⁻ → C₂H₆
mechanism: RCOO⁻ oxidised at anode → RCOO• → loses CO₂ → R• → R• + R• → R-R (radical coupling)
mistake: Confusing with soda lime decarboxylation: soda lime gives RH (lose 1C). Kolbe electrolysis gives R-R (doubled carbons). Thermal = lose. Kolbe = couple.

name: Kharasch Effect (Peroxide Effect)
summary: Kharasch Effect (Peroxide Effect) is the Anti-Markovnikov addition of Hydrogen Bromide ($HBr$) to unsymmetrical alkenes in the presence of organic peroxides. Mechanism: It follows a free-radical chain pathway rather than the usual carbocation path. Requirement: It is strictly observed only with $HBr$. Reactive intermediates: Peroxides generate $Br^\bullet$ radicals which attack the less hindered terminal carbon to form the more stable $2^\circ$ or $3^\circ$ alkyl radical. Note: $HCl$ and $HI$ do not show this effect due to thermodynamic constraints (bond strengths).
mechanism: Free radical addition. Peroxide initiation forms Br radical, which adds to the least substituted carbon.
mistake: Applying it to HCl or HI. Only HBr works with peroxide effect.

name: Swarts Reaction
summary: Swarts Reaction is the standard laboratory method for synthesizing alkyl fluorides through halogen exchange. Reagent: Metallic fluorides such as $AgF$, $Hg_2F_2$, $CoF_2$, or $SbF_3$. Mechanism: An alkyl chloride or bromide is heated with the metallic fluoride to exchange the halogen. Drivers: The reaction is driven by the formation of insoluble metallic halides or the difference in bond enthalpies. Importance: Like Balz-Schiemann for aromatic rings, Swarts is the easiest way to prepare aliphatic fluorides safely.
reagents: Heavy metal fluorides like AgF, Hg2F2, CoF2, SbF3
mechanism: Nucleophilic substitution (halogen exchange).
mistake: Thinking Finkelstein makes alkyl fluorides. Swarts = Fluorides; Finkelstein = Iodides.

name: Gattermann Reaction
summary: Gattermann Reaction is a method to convert arenediazonium salts into aryl halides (chlorides or bromides). Reagents: Copper powder ($Cu$) and the corresponding halogen acid ($HCl$ or $HBr$). Mechanism: Similar to the Sandmeyer reaction, it proceeds via a free-radical pathway where the diazonium group ($N_2^+$) is replaced by a halogen. Comparison: Gattermann is a simpler, 'budget' alternative to Sandmeyer, using copper powder instead of copper(I) salts, often resulting in lower yields but still effective for halogenation of aromatic rings.
reagents: Cu powder / HCl or Cu powder / HBr
mechanism: Free radical mechanism similar to Sandmeyer.
mistake: Confusing Sandmeyer ($CuCl/CuBr$) with Gattermann ($Cu$ powder).

name: Fittig Reaction
summary: Fittig Reaction is a coupling reaction used to synthesize biaryl compounds ($Ar-Ar$) from two molecules of aryl halides ($ArX$). Reagents: Sodium metal ($Na$) in dry ether. Mechanism: It is analogous to the Wurtz reaction but involves aryl halides. It proceeds via a free radical or organosodium intermediate, leading to the formation of a new carbon-carbon bond between two aromatic rings. Distinction: Wurtz couples two alkyl halides, Wurtz-Fittig couples an alkyl and an aryl halide, and Fittig couples two aryl halides.
mechanism: Similar to Wurtz reaction, via radical or organosodium intermediate.
mistake: Mixing up Wurtz (alkyl+alkyl), Fittig (aryl+aryl), Wurtz-Fittig (alkyl+aryl).

name: Tollens' Test
summary: Tollens' Test is a qualitative test for the presence of aldehydes, characterized by the formation of a 'silver mirror'. Reagent: Ammoniacal silver nitrate solution, $[Ag(NH_3)_2]^+OH^-$, a mild oxidizing agent. Mechanism: Aldehydes are oxidized to carboxylate ions, while $Ag^+$ ions are reduced to metallic silver ($Ag^0$), which deposits on the inner surface of the test tube. Scope: Both aliphatic and aromatic aldehydes give a positive test. Exceptions: Ketones generally do not react, but $\alpha$-hydroxy ketones and formic acid ($HCOOH$) can give a positive result.
mechanism: Oxidation of aldehyde by $Ag(NH_3)_2^+$ complex.
mistake: Thinking ketones give a positive test (only alpha-hydroxy ketones do).

name: Fehling's Test
summary: Fehling's Test is a qualitative test primarily used to detect aliphatic aldehydes. Reagents: Fehling's solution, prepared by mixing Fehling A ($CuSO_4$) and Fehling B (alkaline solution of sodium potassium tartrate, Rochelle salt). Mechanism: Aldehydes are oxidized to carboxylate ions, and the blue $Cu^{2+}$ ions are reduced to a brick-red precipitate of cuprous oxide ($Cu_2O$). Distinction: Aromatic aldehydes (e.g., benzaldehyde) typically give a negative test because they are less easily oxidized by this mild reagent, making it useful for distinguishing between aliphatic and aromatic aldehydes.
reactants: Aliphatic aldehydes (aromatic aldehydes give negative test)
mechanism: Oxidation of aldehyde and reduction of $Cu^{2+}$ to $Cu^+$.
mistake: Thinking benzaldehyde gives a positive test. Fehling's only works for ALIPHATIC aldehydes.

name: Hinsberg Test
summary: Hinsberg Test is a chemical test used to distinguish between primary ($1^\circ$), secondary ($2^\circ$), and tertiary ($3^\circ$) amines based on their reactivity with benzenesulfonyl chloride ($C_6H_5SO_2Cl$) and subsequent solubility in alkali. Procedure: The amine reacts with benzenesulfonyl chloride in the presence of $NaOH$. $1^\circ$ amines form an N-substituted sulfonamide which is soluble in $NaOH$ (due to an acidic N-H proton). $2^\circ$ amines form an N,N-disubstituted sulfonamide which is insoluble in $NaOH$ (no acidic N-H proton). $3^\circ$ amines do not react with benzenesulfonyl chloride under these conditions, remaining insoluble (or reacting slowly to form a salt that hydrolyzes).
product: $1^\circ$ gives soluble salt, $2^\circ$ gives insoluble compound, $3^\circ$ does not react.
mechanism: Formation of sulfonamides.
mistake: Forgetting the solubility: $1^\circ$ sulfonamide has an acidic H, so it dissolves in $NaOH$. $2^\circ$ does not.

name: Tiffeneau-Demjanov Rearrangement
summary: Tiffeneau-Demjanov Rearrangement is a ring-expansion method that converts a primary amine on a cyclic 1,2-amino alcohol into a cyclic ketone with one additional carbon. Reagent: Nitrous acid ($HNO_2$ or $NaNO_2/HCl$). Mechanism: Diazotisation of the primary amine group followed by spontaneous loss of $N_2$ gas to trigger a 1,2-migration of a ring carbon. Benefit: It is a more robust alternative to the Pinacol rearrangement for achieving structural expansions from 4 to 5 or 5 to 6 membered rings. Product: An expanded cyclic ketone.
mistake: Similar to Pinacol rearrangement but uses amine and HNO2 instead of diol and H+.

name: Curtius Rearrangement
summary: Curtius Rearrangement is a thermal decomposition method that converts an acyl azide into a primary amine with one fewer carbon. Reagent: Acyl azide ($R-CON_3$), typically prepared from acyl chloride and $NaN_3$. Mechanism: (1) Thermal loss of $N_2$ gas to generate a highly reactive **Acyl Nitrene**. (2) Spontaneous 1,2-shift of the $R$ group to the nitrogen, forming an Isocyanate ($R-N=C=O$). (3) Hydrolysis of the isocyanate yields the amine and $CO_2$. Benefit: Similar to Hofmann Degradation but starts from azides instead of amides.
mechanism: Loss of N2 and simultaneous 1,2-shift of alkyl/aryl group from carbonyl carbon to nitrogen.
mistake: Confusing Hofman, Curtius, Schmidt. Curtius uses acyl AZIDE.

name: Arndt-Eistert Reaction
summary: Arndt-Eistert Synthesis is a homologation method used to convert a carboxylic acid into its next higher homologue ($R-COOH \rightarrow R-CH_2-COOH$). Process: (1) Formation of an acyl chloride. (2) Treatment with Diazomethane ($CH_2N_2$) to form a diazoketone. (3) **Wolff Rearrangement** catalyzed by silver oxide ($Ag_2O$) in the presence of water/alcohol. Key Step: The migration of the R-group to form a Ketene ($R-CH=C=O$) intermediate, which then hydrates to the higher acid.
reactants: Acyl chloride from a carboxylic acid
conditions: Formation of diazoketone, then Wolff rearrangement
mechanism: Diazoketone undergoes Wolff rearrangement to a ketene intermediate, which hydrolyzes.
mistake: Thinking it loses a carbon. It ADDS a CH2 group.

name: Bouveault-Blanc Reduction
summary: Bouveault-Blanc Reduction is the chemical reduction of esters to primary alcohols using sodium metal in absolute ethanol. Reagent: $Na$ / $C_2H_5OH$. Mechanism: It proceeds via a series of Single Electron Transfers (SET) from the sodium metal to the ester carbonyl, followed by protonation from the alcohol. Advantage: Historically important as a source of 1° alcohols before the discovery of $LiAlH_4$. Distinction: Unlike catalytic hydrogenation, it selectively reduces esters even in the presence of some other reducible groups.
mechanism: Single electron transfer (SET) from Na, forming radical anions, followed by protonation from ethanol.
mistake: Thinking LiAlH4 is the only way to reduce esters. Bouveault-Blanc is the classic non-hydride alternative.

name: Elbs-Persulphate Oxidation
summary: Elbs Persulphate Oxidation is the chemical oxidation of phenols to form dihydric phenols (specifically para-diphenols) using alkaline potassium persulphate. Reagent: $K_2S_2O_8$ in aqueous $NaOH$. Mechanism: Nucleophilic attack of the phenoxide oxygen on the persulphate peroxide bond, followed by rearrangement to the para position. Product: Yields Quinol (hydroquinone) after hydrolysis of the intermediate sulphate. Note: If the para position is blocked, oxidation occurs at the ortho position.
mechanism: Electrophilic attack by persulfate on the phenoxide ion, typically at the para position.
mistake: Oxidation does not break the ring; it simply hydroxylates it at the para position.

name: Borodine-Hunsdiecker Reaction
summary: Hunsdiecker Reaction is the decarboxylative bromination of carboxylic acids to form alkyl bromides with one fewer carbon atom. Reagent: Silver salt of the carboxylic acid ($R-COOAg$) and Bromine ($Br_2$) in $CCl_4$. Mechanism: (1) Formation of an acyl hypobromite ($R-COOBr$). (2) Homolytic cleavage to generate an acyl radical ($R-COO^\bullet$). (3) Decarboxylation to form an alkyl radical ($R^\bullet$), which captures bromine. Reactivity: $1^\circ > 2^\circ > 3^\circ$. Note: Using $I_2$ instead of $Br_2$ yields an ester (Simonini reaction).
mechanism: Free radical mechanism: forms acyl hypobromite which loses CO2 to form an alkyl radical that captures Br.
mistake: Writing product with same number of carbons. It loses CO2!

name: Benzidine Rearrangement
summary: Benzidine Rearrangement is the acid-catalyzed conversion of 1,2-diphenylhydrazine into 4,4'-diaminobiphenyl (benzidine). Process: Treatment with strong mineral acids like $HCl$. Mechanism: It is a concerted [5,5]-sigmatropic rearrangement. Logic: Protonation of the hydrazine nitrogens triggers a structural flip where the two para-positions of the aromatic rings couple together, followed by the re-aromatization of the rings. It is a fundamental example of an aromatic rearrangement where a N-N bond is exchanged for a C-C bond.
mechanism: Intramolecular sigmatropic rearrangement.
mistake: Thinking the N-N bond breaks completely free. It is an intramolecular rearrangement.

name: Cope Elimination
summary: Cope Elimination is the 'mild' way to make alkenes without using a strong base. It starts with a Tertiary Amine Oxide, which is then heated. THE MECHANISM: It's a 'Syn-Elimination' that happens through a cyclic 5-membered transition state where the oxygen of the amine oxide reaches around and grabs a beta-hydrogen. Result: You get an alkene and a hydroxylamine. Because it's a 'front-side' attack, it's strictly SYN, unlike the E2 mechanism which is typically ANTI.
mechanism: Concerted syn-elimination through a cyclic 5-membered transition state.
mistake: Confusing with Hofmann elimination which requires base. Cope only needs heat and is SYN.

name: Knoevenagel Reaction
summary: Knoevenagel Condensation is the base-catalyzed nucleophilic addition of an **active methylene compound** to an aldehyde or ketone. Requirement: The methylene compound ($Z-CH_2-Z'$) must be flanked by two electron-withdrawing groups (e.g., malonic ester, acetoacetic ester). Mechanism: Deprotonation forms a stable resonance-stabilized carbanion which attacks the carbonyl. Subsequent dehydration (condensation) yields an $\alpha,\beta$-unsaturated compound. Common Bases: Weak bases like Pyridine or Piperidine are usually sufficient due to the high acidity of the substrate.
reactants: Aldehyde/ketone and active methylene compound (e.g., malonic ester)
mechanism: Enolate of active methylene attacks carbonyl, followed by dehydration.
mistake: Confusing with Aldol. Knoevenagel uses active methylene (two EWGs) and a weak base.

name: Baeyer-Villiger Oxidation
summary: Baeyer-Villiger Oxidation is the conversion of ketones into esters (or cyclic ketones into lactones) using peroxyacids. Reagent: $m-CPBA$, Peracetic acid, or Trifluoroperacetic acid. Mechanism: Nucleophilic attack of the peroxyacid on the ketone, followed by a C-to-O migration of an alkyl/aryl group. Migration Aptitude: $3^\circ > 2^\circ >$ Phenyl $> 1^\circ >$ Methyl. Benefit: Highly regioselective; the more substituted group preferentially migrates to the oxygen. Cyclic products result in ring expansion (e.g., cyclohexanone $\rightarrow \epsilon$-caprolactone).
mechanism: Nucleophilic attack of peracid on carbonyl, followed by rearrangement with alkyl group migration.
stereo: Retention of configuration for the migrating group.
mistake: Migrating the wrong group. Migratory aptitude: H > 3° > 2°/Ph > 1° > Methyl.

name: Libermann Nitroso Reaction
summary: Libermann's Nitroso Test is a colorimetric qualitative test used to detect secondary ($2^\circ$) amines and phenols. Process (for Phenol): Treatment with $NaNO_2$ and conc. $H_2SO_4$. (1) Phenol reacts with nitrous acid to form p-nitrosophenol. (2) This reacts with excess phenol to form **Indophenol**, which is red in acidic medium. (3) On adding $NaOH$, the color changes to blue/green due to the formation of the sodium salt. Sequence: Red (Acid) $\rightarrow$ Blue (Base). Purpose: Distinguishes phenols and $2^\circ$ amines from other functional groups.
conditions: Warming, then dilution, then addition of NaOH
product: Indophenol (blue/green in base, red in acid)
mechanism: Formation of nitrous acid, nitrosation of phenol, followed by condensation with another phenol molecule.
mistake: Confusing the color sequence. Red in acidic dilution, Blue in alkaline.

name: Mendius Reaction
summary: Mendius Reaction is a selective reduction method used to convert nitriles into primary amines. Reagent: Sodium amalgam ($Na(Hg)$) in the presence of boiling ethanol ($C_2H_5OH$). Mechanism: It involves the transfer of four hydrogen atoms via a Single Electron Transfer (SET) process. Value: This reaction is a classic way to achieve 'Carbon Homologation' of an amine, increasing the chain length by one carbon (the cyanide carbon) relative to the starting alkyl group. It delivers pure primary amines without substituted byproducts.
mechanism: Similar to Bouveault-Blanc, SET reduction.
mistake: LiAlH4 also does this, but Mendius specifically refers to Na/EtOH.

name: Tischenko Reaction
summary: Tischenko Reaction is an Al-alkoxide catalyzed disproportionation of aldehydes into esters. Reagent: Aluminum ethoxide ($Al(OC_2H_5)_3$). Distinction: Unlike the Cannizzaro reaction, which specifically requires aldehydes lacking $\alpha$-hydrogens, the Tischenko reaction works on almost all aldehydes. Mechanism: Two molecules of aldehyde disproportionate, but the resulting alcohol and acid intermediate are immediately coupled by the catalyst to form an ester. It is effectively an anhydrous version of the Cannizzaro-Esterification sequence.
mechanism: Coordination to Al followed by hydride transfer between two aldehyde units.
mistake: Cannizzaro requires NO alpha-H. Tischenko works with ANY aldehyde.

name: Ulmann Reaction
summary: Ullmann Reaction is a coupling method for synthesizing symmetrical biaryls ($Ar-Ar$) from aryl halides. Reagent: Finely divided copper powder ($Cu$) at high temperatures. Requirement: Aryl iodides are the most reactive substrates. Aryl bromides and chlorides only react successfully if they are activated by strong electron-withdrawing groups in the ortho/para positions. Intermediate: It proceeds via an arylcopper(I) species. Importance: It is the aromatic sibling of the Wurtz reaction, specifically tuned for biaryl synthesis.
mechanism: Radical mechanism or via organocopper intermediates.
mistake: Using Aryl Chloride without activating groups. Typically needs Aryl Iodide.

name: Ozonolysis
summary: Ozonolysis is a powerful oxidative cleavage of C-C multiple bonds using ozone ($O_3$). Process: Formation of a cyclic molozonide which rearranges to a stable ozonide. The Workup Dictates the Product: (1) **Reductive Workup** ($Zn/H_2O$ or $Me_2S$) stops at aldehydes/ketones. (2) **Oxidative Workup** ($H_2O_2$) converts aldehydes into carboxylic acids. Purpose: This reaction is the primary diagnostic tool in organic chemistry for locating the exact position of double or triple bonds in an unknown hydrocarbon.
mechanism: Cycloaddition forming primary ozonide, rearrangement to stable ozonide, then cleavage.
mistake: Forgetting the workup type. Zn/H2O means STOP at aldehyde. H2O2 means GO to carboxylic acid.

name: Oxymercuration-Demercuration
summary: Oxymercuration-Demercuration is a two-step method for the Markovnikov hydration of alkenes without carbocation rearrangement. Process: (1) Reaction with mercuric acetate ($Hg(OAc)_2$) to form a cyclic mercurinium ion, followed by water attack at the more substituted carbon. (2) Reduction with $NaBH_4$ to remove the mercury. Advantage: Since a free carbocation is never formed, structural rearrangements are suppressed, making this superior to standard acid-catalyzed hydration for branched alkenes.
mechanism: Cyclic mercurinium ion prevents rearrangement. Water attacks the more substituted carbon (anti-addition).
mistake: Rearranging the skeleton as in acid-catalyzed hydration.

name: Hydroboration - Oxidation
summary: Hydroboration-Oxidation is a regioselective method for the Anti-Markovnikov hydration of alkenes. Reagents: Borane ($BH_3$) or Diborane ($B_2H_6$) followed by oxidation with alkaline $H_2O_2$. Stereochemistry: It is a concerted **Syn-addition** of H and B across the double bond. Regiochemistry: The bulky boron group selectively attaches to the least hindered carbon. Result: A primary or secondary alcohol with configuration retention. Note: This reaction specifically yields the 'Anti-Markovnikov' product which is impossible via direct acid hydration.
mechanism: Concerted syn-addition of B and H, placing B on the less sterically hindered carbon, followed by oxidation with retention of configuration.
mistake: Markovnikov addition instead of Anti-Markovnikov, or getting the stereochemistry wrong.

name: Hoffmann Elimination
summary: Hofmann Elimination is the base-promoted E2 elimination of quaternary ammonium salts that selectively yields the 'Least Substituted' alkene. Procedure: (1) Conversion of an amine to an iodide salt. (2) Treatment with moist silver oxide ($Ag_2O/H_2O$) to form a quaternary hydroxide. (3) Thermal elimination. Thermodynamic Driver: The reaction is controlled by steric effects; the bulky $-NR_3^+$ leaving group forces the base to extract a proton from the most accessible (least hindered) methyl group. Result: The Hofmann product (anti-Saytzeff).
mechanism: E2 elimination. Bulky leaving group and strong base character drive formation of less sterically hindered carbanion-like TS.
mistake: Forming the Saytzeff (more substituted) product. Hofmann's rule prefers the less substituted alkene.

name: Claisen Rearrangement
summary: Claisen Rearrangement is a purely thermal [3,3]-sigmatropic rearrangement that converts an allyl aryl ether into an ortho-allyl phenol. Process: Heating the substrate (typically $>200^\circ C$) without a catalyst. Mechanism: A concerted pericyclic [3,3]-shift through a six-membered cyclic transition state. Inversion Effect: The allyl group 'inverts' during the migration—the terminal $\gamma$-carbon of the allyl chain becomes attached to the ortho carbon of the ring. If both ortho positions are blocked, a second rearrangement to the para position may occur (Para-Claisen).
mechanism: Concerted [3,3]-sigmatropic rearrangement through a 6-membered cyclic transition state.
stereo: Retention of configuration regarding the alkene if substituents are present.
mistake: Not realizing the allyl group INVERTS. If labeled, the end that was free attaches to the ring.
